topic: An Introduction to Philosophy of Religion
subject: religious-studies

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.correct: The Islamic declaration of faith: 'There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the messenger of Allah' — affirming monotheism and Muhammad's prophethood.
items.misconception: The Shahada is the requirement to pray five times a day.
items.random_wrong: The Shahada is the Islamic pilgrimage to Mecca.
items.why: Students confuse the five pillars. Shahada is the declaration, Salah is prayer, Hajj is pilgrimage — each is a distinct pillar with a different function.

items.q: Which encyclical banned Catholic contraception in 1968?
items.why: It's Humanae Vitae. “Rerum Novarum” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Maximum wives allowed under Islamic law?
items.why: The real figure is 4 wives. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
